---
title: "Quiz 6: Two-Way ANOVA & Interaction"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## About this quiz
**Module:** Two-Way ANOVA & Interaction  ·  **Available after:** Week 8  ·  **Source:** Lecture Notes, [Ch. 5 (Interactions)](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html), with background from [Ch. 3 (One-Way ANOVA)](../../notes/chapters/03-one-way-anova.html).

This quiz assesses the two-way ANOVA with two categorical predictors: what the design tests (two main effects plus their interaction), reading the main- and interaction-$F$-tests with their degrees of freedom from a Type-III table, why a significant interaction makes the main effects incomplete summaries, reading an interaction plot and quantifying a simple effect and a difference of differences from a cell-means table, the equivalence between the two-way ANOVA and a dummy-plus-product regression, and interpreting conditional (simple) effects rather than a single main effect. Mirroring Homework 6, every item uses the PISA reading example — students from the USA, UK, and Germany ($N = 4{,}528$) crossed with whether the student speaks the test language at home — and emphasizes interpretation over computation. Items are open-book; every table, equation, and result is shown statically, and each item carries the output it needs in its own stem.
:::

## Blackboard settings

| Setting | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| Number of questions | 11 |
| Total points | 16 |
| Time limit | 60 minutes |
| Attempts | Unlimited |
| Score recorded | Highest |
| Availability | After Week 8 |
| Show correct answers | After the availability window closes |

: Deployment settings for Quiz 6. Time limit and answer-release timing are proposed defaults (see the [Blackboard Setup](../appendices/blackboard-setup.qmd) appendix). {.striped}

## Questions

### Question 1 — What a two-way ANOVA tests {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 5]`**

Researchers model PISA reading literacy score (`wleread`) on **country** (USA, UK, Germany) and whether the student **speaks the test language at home** (No, Yes) using a two-way ANOVA with the country-by-test-language interaction. Their central question is whether the boost from speaking the test language at home is the *same size* in every country. Which statement best describes what this single analysis tests?

A. It tests two main effects (country and test language) plus their interaction, and the interaction — does the test-language effect differ by country? — is the key test for their question.

B. It runs two separate one-way ANOVAs, one for country and one for test language, and reports them side by side.

C. It tests the two main effects, while the interaction is an optional add-on that is examined only if both main effects are non-significant.

D. It tests the two main effects as the primary result; the interaction merely re-scales the main effects and need not be interpreted.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: A**

- **Why A is correct:** A two-way ANOVA partitions the outcome into a main effect for each factor *plus* their interaction, all estimated in one model. The interaction term is exactly the test of whether the test-language effect changes across countries — the researchers' central question — so it is the key test here, not an afterthought.
- **Why not B:** Two separate one-way ANOVAs cannot estimate the interaction at all; fitting both factors jointly in one model is what lets the "does it depend on country?" question be tested.
- **Why not C:** The interaction is not contingent on the main effects being non-significant. It is tested directly and is often the primary result; here it is significant even though both main effects are too.
- **Why not D:** A significant interaction does not "re-scale" the main effects — it tells you the effect of one factor is not constant across the other, which *changes how* the main effects may be read rather than leaving them untouched.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Categorical by Categorical Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#sec-ch5-cat-cat).

:::

### Question 2 — Reading the Type-III interaction test {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 5]`**

The two-way ANOVA of reading score on country $\times$ test language ($N = 4{,}528$) produces the Type-III table below.

| Source | df | $F$ | $p$ |
|:-------|---:|----:|:----|
| Test language | 1 | 59.57 | $<.001$ |
| Country | 2 | 26.00 | $<.001$ |
| Test language $\times$ Country | ? | ? | $<.001$ |
| Residual | 4522 | | |

Read the **interaction** row and complete the report at $\alpha = .05$:

"The Country $\times$ Test-language interaction is tested by $F =$ **[blank1]** on **[blank2]** numerator degrees of freedom and **[blank3]** denominator degrees of freedom; because $p < .001$, the interaction is **[blank4]** (significant / not significant)."

Enter the $F$ value and each df as a single number, and the significance verdict as one word.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answers:** `[blank1]` = **14.72** (accept 14.6–14.8, tolerance $\pm 0.1$) · `[blank2]` = **2** (numerator df; exact) · `[blank3]` = **4522** (denominator df; exact) · `[blank4]` = **significant**

- **`[blank1]` = 14.72:** the interaction $F$ for these data, read straight from the Test language $\times$ Country row, $F(2, 4522) = 14.72$.
- **`[blank2]` = 2:** the interaction numerator df is $(\text{#country levels}-1)(\text{#test-language levels}-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$ — one fewer than the number of countries, *not* the number of countries.
- **`[blank3]` = 4522:** the denominator df is the residual df, $4522$, read from the Residual row of the table.
- **`[blank4]` = significant:** with $p < .001 < .05$, reject the null of no interaction — the test-language effect is *not* the same across countries.
- **Common errors:** entering `3` (the number of countries, $k$ instead of $k-1$) or `1` (the test-language main-effect df) for the numerator df, putting the residual df $4522$ in the numerator blank (swapping the two df), or reading a sum-of-squares value as the $F$; all fall outside the keyed answers.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Testing the Overall Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#testing-the-overall-interaction).

:::

### Question 3 — A significant interaction makes main effects incomplete {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 5]`**

In the PISA model the country $\times$ test-language interaction is significant, $F(2, 4522) = 14.72$, $p < .001$, and both main effects are significant as well.

**True or False:** Because each factor still has a significant main effect, it is fine to report a single average test-language effect as "the" effect of speaking the test language at home, summarizing all three countries at once.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B (False)**

- **Why B (False) is correct:** A significant interaction means the test-language effect is **not constant** across countries — it is large in Germany ($\approx +98.5$ points), moderate in the USA ($\approx +62.9$), and near zero in the UK. Once the effect differs by country, a single pooled "average effect" hides exactly the variation the interaction flagged, so the main effect becomes an **incomplete summary**. The honest report gives the effect country by country.
- **Why "True" is wrong:** The significance of the main-effect $F$ does not rescue a single-number summary; the marginal effect still exists arithmetically, but presenting it *as if it applied uniformly* would misrepresent the UK (where the effect is $\approx 0$) and Germany (where it is $\approx +99$) alike. That additive reading is valid only when the interaction is negligible.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Categorical by Categorical Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#sec-ch5-cat-cat).

:::

### Question 4 — Reading the interaction plot {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Applied · Notes Ch. 5]`**

An interaction plot puts test-language status (No, Yes) on the horizontal axis and estimated mean reading score on the vertical axis, drawing one line per country. The **Germany** line rises steeply (about $423$ up to $522$), the **USA** line rises moderately (about $466$ up to $529$), and the **UK** line stays essentially **flat** (about $538$ at both points). What does this pattern indicate?

A. No interaction, because all three lines slope upward in the same general direction.

B. The vertical gaps between the lines reflect only the country intercepts and say nothing about an interaction.

C. An interaction: the lines are **not parallel**, so the test-language difference (each line's slope) changes across countries — steep for Germany, flat for the UK.

D. No interaction can be claimed unless the lines actually cross each other on the plot.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: C**

- **Why C is correct:** On an interaction plot, the slope of each line *is* that country's test-language effect. Germany's steep rise, the USA's moderate rise, and the UK's flat line are three different slopes — the lines are **non-parallel**, the visual signature of an interaction. They also converge on the "Yes" side (the country means are far apart among non-speakers but close among speakers), and that convergence is the interaction made visible.
- **Why not A:** Lines can all point "upward" yet have very different steepness; shared *direction* is not the criterion. Non-parallelism — differing slopes — is what signals an interaction, and these slopes plainly differ.
- **Why not B:** Differing intercepts (baseline gaps) are a *main effect* of country. The interaction is read from whether the lines are *parallel*; dismissing the changing slopes as "intercept only" misreads the plot.
- **Why not D:** Crossing is sufficient but not necessary. Any departure from parallel — including converging lines that never fully cross, as here — indicates an interaction.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Interpreting the Cell Means"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#interpreting-the-cell-means).

:::

### Question 5 — A simple effect from the cell means {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-the-Blank · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 5]`**

The estimated mean reading scores in each country-by-test-language cell are:

| Country | Does **not** speak test language (No) | Speaks test language (Yes) |
|:--------|--------------------------------------:|---------------------------:|
| Germany | 423.40 | 521.93 |
| USA     | 465.77 | 528.63 |
| UK      | 538.19 | 538.18 |

Compute the **simple effect of test language within Germany** — the predicted difference in reading score between German students who *do* speak the test language at home and those who do *not* (Yes − No). Enter a number (reading-score points), rounded to two decimals.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: 98.53** (accept **98.0–99.0**, tolerance $\pm 0.5$)

- **Why 98.53:** the simple effect within a country is the within-row difference of its two cell means, $521.93 - 423.40 = 98.53$ points. German students who speak the test language at home are predicted to score about **98.5 points higher** in reading than those who do not — the largest test-language effect of the three countries.
- **Tolerance:** the $\pm 0.5$ window absorbs rounding of the cell means without admitting a wrong cell or a reversed direction.
- **Common errors:** answering `62.87` (the *USA* simple effect, $528.63 - 465.77$), `≈ 0` (the *UK* effect, $538.18 - 538.19$), or `−98.53` (subtracting No − Yes, the wrong direction — speakers score higher, so the effect is positive); all fall outside the window.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Interpreting the Cell Means"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#interpreting-the-cell-means).

:::

### Question 6 — The interaction as a difference of differences {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Applied · Notes Ch. 5]`**

Using the same cell-means table:

| Country | Does **not** speak test language (No) | Speaks test language (Yes) |
|:--------|--------------------------------------:|---------------------------:|
| Germany | 423.40 | 521.93 |
| USA     | 465.77 | 528.63 |
| UK      | 538.19 | 538.18 |

Read the interaction as a *difference of differences*. The test-language simple effect (Yes − No) is $521.93 - 423.40 = 98.53$ in Germany and $538.18 - 538.19 \approx 0$ in the UK. By how much does Germany's test-language effect exceed the UK's, and what does that quantity represent?

A. About $98.5$ points, but this is just Germany's simple effect on its own and is unrelated to the interaction.

B. About $98.5$ points — the difference between Germany's test-language effect ($\approx 98.5$) and the UK's ($\approx 0$); this contrast of two simple effects is exactly what the interaction quantifies.

C. About $538$ points — the difference between the two countries' reading scores among speakers.

D. About $98.5$ points, obtained by *adding* Germany's effect ($\approx 98.5$) and the UK's ($\approx 0$).

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B**

- **Why B is correct:** An interaction is a *difference of differences*. Germany's test-language effect ($98.53$) minus the UK's ($\approx 0$) is $98.53 - 0 \approx 98.5$ points — how much *more* the test-language effect matters in Germany than in the UK. That contrast between two simple effects is precisely what the interaction quantifies; a zero difference of differences would mean no interaction.
- **Why not A:** The numeric value here *coincides* with Germany's standalone simple effect only because the UK effect is $\approx 0$. But the *quantity* being computed is the difference between two countries' simple effects — that difference is the interaction. Calling it "just Germany's effect, unrelated to the interaction" names the right number under the wrong reasoning.
- **Why not C:** About $538$ is a single cell value (a country contrast among speakers), not a difference of test-language effects.
- **Why not D:** A difference of differences *subtracts* one simple effect from the other; it does not sum them. The sum reasoning would mislead whenever the second effect is not $\approx 0$.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Interpreting the Cell Means"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#interpreting-the-cell-means).

:::

### Question 7 — Two-way ANOVA equals dummy-plus-product regression {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 5]`**

The PISA two-way ANOVA (`wleread ~ test_lan * country`) is re-estimated as a regression on dummy variables plus their products — the two country dummies (`uk`, `germany`), the test-language dummy (`test_lan`), and the two interaction products — with USA non-speakers as the reference cell. The regression reports overall $F = 31.49$, $R^2 = 0.0336$, and Root MSE $= 93.24$.

**True or False:** This dummy-plus-product regression is the *same model* as the two-way ANOVA — it returns identical fitted values, the same $R^2$, the same Root MSE, and the same overall $F$ — just written in regression notation.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: A (True)**

- **Why A (True) is correct:** A two-way ANOVA with an interaction **is** a regression with dummy variables and their product terms — the same design matrix written two ways. It therefore produces identical fitted cell means and the same global fit statistics: $R^2 = 0.0336$, Root MSE $= 93.24$, and overall $F = 31.49$. The ANOVA table simply groups the dummy and product coefficients into factor-level $F$-tests; the regression shows the individual coefficients.
- **Why "False" is wrong:** Claiming the two give *different* fits — or that only the $F$ matches while $R^2$/Root MSE differ, or that the predictors must first be *centered* — misstates the equivalence. Centering can change which conditional value a lower-order coefficient reports, but it does **not** alter the fitted values or any of these global fit statistics, and no centering is needed here because dummy coding already places the reference cell at the intercept.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Categorical by Categorical Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#sec-ch5-cat-cat).

:::

### Question 8 — Mapping regression coefficients to cell means {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 5]`**

The dummy-plus-product regression (USA non-speakers = reference cell) returns:

$$
\widehat{\text{wleread}} = 465.77 + 72.42\,(\text{uk}) - 42.37\,(\text{germany}) + 62.87\,(\text{test\_lan}) + 35.66\,(\text{lang\_germ}) - 62.87\,(\text{lang\_uk})
$$

where $\text{lang\_germ} = \text{test\_lan} \times \text{germany}$ and $\text{lang\_uk} = \text{test\_lan} \times \text{uk}$. Identify the **value** each coefficient takes, given what it represents. Enter each blank as a number.

- The **intercept** is the predicted reading score for **USA students who do not speak the test language at home** (the reference cell); its value is **[blank1]**.
- The **`uk`** coefficient is the **UK − USA** difference in mean reading score **among non-speakers**; its value is **[blank2]**.
- The **`test_lan`** coefficient is the test-language effect (Yes − No) **in the USA**, the reference country; its value is **[blank3]**.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answers:** `[blank1]` = **465.77** (accept 465.3–466.3, tolerance $\pm 0.5$) · `[blank2]` = **72.42** (accept 71.9–72.9) · `[blank3]` = **62.87** (accept 62.4–63.4)

- **`[blank1]` = 465.77:** with every dummy set to $0$ (USA, non-speaker) the fitted value is the intercept — the reference-cell mean, USA non-speakers. It is *not* the grand mean.
- **`[blank2]` = 72.42:** in a model with the interaction, `uk` is the UK − USA gap evaluated where `test_lan` $= 0$ — i.e., **among non-speakers**: $538.19 - 465.77 = 72.42$. It is a conditional, not an overall, country gap.
- **`[blank3]` = 62.87:** `test_lan` is the test-language effect for the reference country, the **USA**: $528.63 - 465.77 = 62.87$. It is *not* "everyone's" effect, because the interaction terms adjust it for the other countries.
- **Common error:** treating the intercept as the grand mean, or reading `uk`/`test_lan` as overall (marginal) effects rather than effects conditional on the other factor being at its reference level.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Interpreting the Cell Means"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#interpreting-the-cell-means).

:::

### Question 9 — Recovering a simple effect from the regression {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-the-Blank · 2 pts · Challenge · Notes Ch. 5]`**

From the same regression (USA non-speakers = reference):

$$
\widehat{\text{wleread}} = 465.77 + 72.42\,(\text{uk}) - 42.37\,(\text{germany}) + 62.87\,(\text{test\_lan}) + 35.66\,(\text{lang\_germ}) - 62.87\,(\text{lang\_uk})
$$

Here `test_lan` $= 62.87$ is the test-language effect for the **USA** only, and the product term `lang_germ` $= 35.66$ is **how much larger** Germany's test-language effect is than the USA's. Using these coefficients, recover **Germany's** test-language (Yes − No) simple effect. Enter a number (reading-score points), rounded to two decimals.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: 98.53** (accept **98.0–99.0**, tolerance $\pm 0.5$)

- **Why 98.53:** a country's simple effect from the regression is the reference-country effect plus that country's interaction term, $\beta_{\text{test\_lan}} + \beta_{\text{lang\_germ}} = 62.87 + 35.66 = 98.53$ points. This exactly recovers Germany's cell-mean difference, $521.93 - 423.40 = 98.53$ — the regression and the cell means tell the same story.
- **Tolerance:** the $\pm 0.5$ window absorbs rounding of the two coefficients while excluding the common wrong combinations.
- **Common errors:** answering `35.66` (the interaction dummy alone — the *amount by which* Germany's effect exceeds the USA's, not Germany's full effect), `62.87` (the USA effect, ignoring Germany's adjustment), or `27.21` (subtracting, $62.87 - 35.66$, instead of adding the same-signed adjustment); all fall outside the window.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Deriving Group-Specific Equations"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#deriving-group-specific-equations).

:::

### Question 10 — Interpreting effects when the interaction is significant {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Challenge · Notes Ch. 5]`**

The country $\times$ test-language interaction is significant. A colleague asks you to state, in one sentence, "the effect of speaking the test language at home on reading score." What is the statistically sound response?

A. Report the single pooled test-language main effect as "the" effect, since its $F$-test is significant and one number is the most concise summary.

B. Average the three country-specific effects into one overall number and report that average, treating the interaction as noise.

C. Report the per-country (simple) effects — about $+98.5$ points in Germany, $+62.9$ in the USA, and $\approx 0$ in the UK — because a significant interaction means the effect depends on country, so you must specify "the effect for whom / where?"

D. Report only Germany's effect (the largest, at $\approx +98.5$), since the largest simple effect best represents the variable's overall importance.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: C**

- **Why C is correct:** When the interaction is significant, the test-language effect is *conditional* on country, so the correct unit of interpretation is the **simple (conditional) effect**: $\approx +98.5$ in Germany, $+62.9$ in the USA, $\approx 0$ in the UK. The discipline is to ask "the effect for whom, or under what condition?" — a single number cannot answer that honestly.
- **Why not A:** Reporting the pooled main effect as "the" effect ignores the very heterogeneity the significant interaction established; it would imply a sizeable effect in the UK, where there is essentially none.
- **Why not B:** Averaging *over* the interaction collapses the differences back into one number — the same error as A in disguise — and describes no actual country well. The point of finding an interaction is to *stop* reporting a single average.
- **Why not D:** The largest simple effect is not "representative"; it is one of three genuinely different effects. Reporting only Germany would misstate the USA and badly misstate the UK.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Categorical by Categorical Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#sec-ch5-cat-cat).

:::

### Question 11 — Matching terms to two-way components {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Matching · 2 pts · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 5]`**

In the PISA two-way ANOVA of reading score on country $\times$ test language, match each quantity from the analysis (left) to the model component it represents (right). Each component is used at most once; one right-column option is a distractor.

| # | Quantity |
|:--|:---------|
| 1 | The $F(1, 4522) = 59.57$ test for **test language** |
| 2 | The $F(2, 4522) = 26.00$ test for **country** |
| 3 | The $F(2, 4522) = 14.72$ test for **country $\times$ test language** |
| 4 | The predicted value $521.93$ for **German students who speak the test language** |

| | Component |
|:--|:---------|
| W | A main effect (one factor, averaging over the other) |
| X | A second main effect (the other factor, averaging over the first) |
| Y | The interaction (whether one factor's effect depends on the other) |
| Z | A single cell mean (one country $\times$ test-language combination's fitted mean) |
| V | A three-way interaction among three factors |

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct pairing: 1 → W, 2 → X, 3 → Y, 4 → Z**

- **1 → W:** the test-language $F$-test is a **main effect** — does mean reading score differ between speakers and non-speakers, averaging over country.
- **2 → X:** the country $F$-test is the **other main effect** — does mean reading score differ across the three countries, averaging over test language.
- **3 → Y:** the country $\times$ test-language $F$-test is the **interaction** — does the test-language effect differ across countries.
- **4 → Z:** $521.93$ is a single **cell mean**, the fitted value for one country-by-test-language combination (German speakers), not an effect or a test.
- **Distractor V (three-way interaction):** unused. This design has only **two** factors, so no three-way interaction exists; a three-way term would require a third categorical factor crossed with these two.
- **Source:** [Ch. 5 (Interactions), "Testing the Overall Interaction"](../../notes/chapters/05-interactions.html#testing-the-overall-interaction).

:::

## Coverage

This quiz draws entirely from Lecture Notes Ch. 5 (Interactions), with conceptual background from Ch. 3 (One-Way ANOVA), and mirrors Homework 6's PISA country $\times$ test-language two-way ANOVA. The 11 items total **16 points** (Multiple Choice ×4, True/False ×2, Fill-in-the-Blank ×2, Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks ×2, Matching ×1) and span all three difficulty levels (Conceptual ×4, Applied ×5, Challenge ×2).

| # | Concept | Type | Difficulty | Pts |
|:--|:--------|:-----|:-----------|:---:|
| 1 | What a two-way ANOVA tests (two main effects + interaction) | Multiple Choice | Conceptual | 1 |
| 2 | Read the interaction $F$, df, and decision from a Type-III table | Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks | Applied | 2 |
| 3 | Significant interaction ⇒ main effects are incomplete | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |
| 4 | Reading an interaction plot (non-parallel ⇒ interaction) | Multiple Choice | Applied | 1 |
| 5 | A simple effect from the cell means (Germany 98.53) | Fill-in-the-Blank | Applied | 2 |
| 6 | The interaction as a difference of differences | Multiple Choice | Applied | 1 |
| 7 | Two-way ANOVA ≡ dummy-plus-product regression | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |
| 8 | Map regression coefficients to cell means | Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks | Applied | 2 |
| 9 | Recover a simple effect from the regression (62.87 + 35.66 = 98.53) | Fill-in-the-Blank | Challenge | 2 |
| 10 | Interpret conditional effects, not a single main effect | Multiple Choice | Challenge | 1 |
| 11 | Match terms to two-way components | Matching | Conceptual | 2 |

: Quiz 6 item map. {.striped}
